---
title: "tracker_explore"
author: "Holden Jones"
date: "2026-04-21"
output: html_document
---

Load packages
```{r}
library(baseballr)
library(dplyr)
library(jsonlite)
library(purr)
```

load game_log
```{r}
game_log <- read_csv("data/processed_data/game_log.csv")
```

define game_ID searching function
```{r}
# 1. The ID Finder Function
get_id <- function(game_date, team) {
  # Standardize date
  fmt_date <- as.character(as.Date(game_date))
  
  # Fetch games from MLB API
  day_data <- get_game_pks_mlb(date = fmt_date)
  
  # Safety check
  if (is.null(day_data) || nrow(day_data) == 0) return(NA)
  
  # Flatten nested data to access team names
  df_flat <- jsonlite::flatten(day_data)
  
  # Search for the team name in home or away columns
  # This finds "Red Sox" even if the API says "Boston Red Sox"
  idx <- which(
    grepl(team, df_flat$teams.home.team.name, ignore.case = TRUE) | 
    grepl(team, df_flat$teams.away.team.name, ignore.case = TRUE)
  )
  
  if (length(idx) > 0) {
    return(df_flat$game_pk[idx[1]])
  } else {
    return(NA)
  }
}

# 2. Apply it to your game_log dataframe
# Note: This might take a moment as it pings the API for each row
game_log$gameID <- mapply(get_id, game_log$date, game_log$home_team)

# 3. View your updated log
print(game_log)
```

extract all players that played appeared in games I saw
```{r}
# Function to get a clean list of players from a gameID
get_roster <- function(id) {
  if (is.na(id)) return(NULL)
  
  # Get the box score data
  bs <- mlb_boxscore(id)
  
  # Extract names from both teams
  players <- bs %>% 
    select(person_id, person_full_name, team_full_name)
  
  return(players)
}

# This will create a list of data frames containing every player 
# you've seen in the games listed in game_log
all_players_seen <- map(game_log$gameID, get_roster)

# Combine into one big master list of unique players
master_player_list <- bind_rows(all_players_seen) %>% distinct(person_id, .keep_all = TRUE)

head(master_player_list)
```
